direction.one_liner: Humble brutalist designer portfolio: raw document aesthetic with one typographic in-joke

display.family: Distinctive grotesk with monospace leanings — reads like GT America Mono or a similar industrial face
features: moderate size — H2 scale, not oversized hero
display.note:
The author explicitly cites "humble brutalist architecture" as an influence.
The type choice follows: geometric, plain, no flourishes, but a single
deliberate deformation — accented characters — acts as the signature.

typography.notes:
The page behaves like a document, not a landing page. Paragraphs flow with
soft line breaks. Inline links sit inside sentences. This is a CV rendered
as a webpage, not a marketing site dressed as a CV.

color.palette_strategy:
Zero chrome. Black on white plus one functional link color. All visual energy
is displaced onto the embedded media (GIFs and MP4s of real product work),
which is where credibility lives in a designer portfolio anyway.

signature_moves: Full-bleed GIFs and MP4s of real shipped product work (Instagram Shops localization, Farfetch navigation)
Case studies written as short paragraphs — role, scope, outcome in one breath
Long tiled strip of album covers at the bottom as a personal signal
Email address as the primary CTA via mailto: link

techniques: autoplaying GIFs and inline video carry the demonstration
no scroll orchestration, no reveal animations

signature_detail:
The typographic in-joke in the section title "Intêrnatiønal Cømmęrče" —
extensive diacritics scattered across the word. It is simultaneously a visual
ornament and a direct commentary on the case study's subject (localization
across languages and scripts). It is the only decorative element on the
entire site, and because it is the only one, it functions as the author's
signature.

stack_guess.evidence: All asset URLs route through framerusercontent.com. The duplicated HTML blocks in the source correspond to Framer's responsive breakpoint rendering.

works_for: Personal portfolios of designers, engineers, or technical writers
Independent consultants or senior ICs whose work should speak for itself
CV-style pages where credibility outweighs conversion
avoid_for: Product marketing sites — no conversion rhythm
Teams that need to signal scale and stack

notes:
This is the antithesis of Crusoe and DCVC. Those sites construct institutional
authority. This one constructs personal credibility — and does so precisely
by refusing to package itself as an agency would.

Core lesson: if you build a personal site, do not imitate agency polish.
Stay at the document level, let the work embed directly, and add exactly one
typographic signature somewhere unexpected. The restraint is what reads as
confidence.

direction.one_liner: Industrial-editorial infrastructure: isometric SVG over white with a single electric-yellow punctuation mark

display.family: Custom geometric sans, condensed proportions (reads as bespoke or licensed — not system)
display.note: The headline typeface is the first signal of budget and seriousness; it is clearly not a free Google font.

typography.notes:
Hero headlines do the heavy lifting. Body copy stays small, technical, almost
datasheet-like. The contrast between a 100px headline and 14px caption is the
rhythm of the whole page.

accents.name: signal-yellow
accents.role: single sharp accent — square dots, highlights inside illustrations, cursor for key CTAs

color.palette_strategy:
Dominant black-on-white with one electric accent used as punctuation, not area.
If the yellow were deployed as a background fill anywhere the whole composition
would collapse into dev-tool cliche. The discipline is the design.

layout.grid: 12col with large hero blocks and numbered (01–04) stacked product sections
signature_moves: Numbered sections (01 Managed AI / 02 Cloud / 03 Data Centers / 04 Energy) that scroll-reveal vertically, turning a broad stack into a narrative
Isometric SVG illustrations of data centers, microgrids, and server racks instead of stock renders
Full-width logo carousel with high-signal customers (Cursor, Windsurf, HeyGen)
Sticky headers that swap the adjacent illustration as you scroll

techniques: sticky title swaps illustration as user scrolls past
carousel of model chips with prev/next arrows
motion.library_guess: Webflow native interactions, likely with a GSAP ScrollTrigger layer

backgrounds: muted grey as the only section break
photographic avatars only inside testimonial cards

signature_detail:
The isometric SVG illustrations with yellow square accents. They communicate
"we operate physical infrastructure" without a single stock photo, and the
numbered 01–04 rhythm converts a four-product portfolio into a single vertical
narrative. Nothing else on the page is louder than the illustration, and the
illustration itself is monochrome except for the yellow squares — which is
exactly why the yellow reads as voltage.

stack_guess.evidence: Asset CDN paths use website-files.com and prod.website-files.com — Webflow's signature asset pipeline. Asset IDs follow Webflow's hash naming pattern.

works_for: B2B infrastructure and developer-platform companies with enterprise buyers
Hardware-adjacent or deep-tech companies that need to feel solid
Companies with a portfolio of 3–5 products (numbered narrative works)
Series C+ stage where restraint reads as confidence
avoid_for: Consumer products that need warmth or personality
Early-stage scrappy startups — this aesthetic reads as institutional
Single-product companies (the numbered-stack move has nothing to chew on)

notes:
Authority is built from three moves: (1) numbered product structure that
narrates rather than lists, (2) custom isometric illustrations in place of
stock renders, (3) customer logos (Cursor, Windsurf, HeyGen) placed inside
a section header rather than relegated to a marketing strip.

The yellow works because it is rare. On the scale of the viewport it probably
occupies <1% of painted pixels. A 30% increase in yellow coverage would push
the site into hackathon-poster territory.

direction.one_liner: Editorial deep-tech VC: serif gravitas, italic punch-lines, print-magazine pacing

display.family: High-contrast transitional serif — reads like GT Sectra, Tiempos Headline, or Canela
features: italic emphasis inside headlines ('hardest, highest-stakes problems' set italic)
generous size, full hero statement rather than a tagline

body.family: Neutral humanist sans — Sohne, Graphik, or National 2 family

typography.notes:
The defining typographic move is italic injection. A serif display face with
selective italics inside the headline signals "there is a point of view here,
this is an essay not a pitch." It is the single most efficient way to buy
editorial credibility in a web context.

color.palette_strategy:
Near-monochrome warm paper palette. Color is not a tool here — typography is.
The single brand blue reads as a printed ink color rather than a UI primary.

layout.grid: editorial: wide margins, clear text column, card grids for portfolio highlights
signature_moves: Hero video auto-plays with an explicit Pause/Play control, signaling content not spam
Spotlight companies rotate as richly described cards, each with an investment date
Content Topics taxonomy in the footer (Climate, TechBio, Space, Defense) scoped like a publication
Standalone typographic quote-blocks used as breath between sections

techniques: ambient hero video with manual pause
motion.library_guess: Custom/jQuery-era implementation; no heavy motion library is visible

signature_detail:
Italic injection inside serif display type. The phrase "hardest, highest-stakes
problems" set in italic serif at display size carries the entire hero —
no illustrations, no gradients, no decorative elements. It is a pull-quote
treatment applied to the headline itself, and it tells the reader in one
glance that this is a publisher, not a dashboard.

stack_guess.evidence: Image paths follow /imager/uploads/ — the signature of Craft CMS with the Imager plugin. Markup is server-rendered rather than hydrated.

works_for: Venture funds, research labs, think tanks
Long-form content brands (newsletters, publishers, media companies)
B2B companies that need to read as serious and intellectual
avoid_for: Fast-moving consumer products needing immediate warmth
Developer tools where scan-ability beats reading
Anything that needs to feel young, playful, or casual

notes:
The site is the work of One Design Company, a San Francisco studio that also
designs books. This explains the print sensibility — they carry typographic
habits from physical formats into the browser.

The most transferable lesson: selective italics inside a serif headline buy
"writer with a point of view" at very low cost. One font weight, one character
style change, and the whole register of the page shifts.
